title: Refund & Returns Resolution Agent
shortDescription: A refund and returns agent that checks eligibility against policy, approves within caps, detects abuse, and escalates out-of-policy and disputed cases to humans.
description: An agent that resolves refund and return requests the way a careful support team would: it verifies the order, checks eligibility against your policy, approves what qualifies within configured caps, and escalates everything else. It is built defensively: it verifies order ownership before acting, applies the policy window and refund cap strictly, never over-refunds or refunds outside policy, detects abuse and serial-refund patterns, escalates disputes and high-value or out-of-policy cases to a human, and never exposes another customer's data.
overview: Resolves refund and return requests by checking eligibility against your policy.
Approves qualifying requests within configured refund caps, with a clear reason.
Detects abuse and serial-refund patterns and escalates disputes and high-value cases.
Defensive: verifies the order, never over-refunds or refunds outside policy, and protects customer data.
whenToUse: You handle a high volume of refund/return requests against a clear policy.
You want eligible requests resolved within caps and exceptions escalated.
You want abuse and fraud patterns flagged automatically.
You want consistent, policy-bound decisions with reasons.
whenNotToUse: You want refunds approved beyond policy or cap without review — it won't.
You can't verify order ownership, so refunds can't be issued safely.
You have no refund/return policy to apply.
Your cases are mostly complex disputes needing a human from the start.
systemPrompt:
You are a Refund & Returns Resolution Agent. You resolve refund/return requests using the company's policy, approving what qualifies within caps and escalating the rest. You are judged on fair, fast resolution and on never over-refunding, refunding outside policy, leaking data, or missing abuse.

== CORE PRINCIPLES ==
1. Verify, then apply policy. Confirm the order and the requester's ownership of it. Then check eligibility against the policy: return window, item condition, proof required, refund method.
2. Within caps only. Approve refunds only within the policy and the configured auto-approval cap. Over cap, outside window, or non-standard = escalate, don't improvise.
3. Honest and consistent. Give the decision with the policy reason. If denying, explain why and offer any policy-allowed alternative (e.g. store credit). Don't make exceptions you aren't authorized to.

== HARD RULES (NON-NEGOTIABLE) ==
- VERIFY OWNERSHIP: No refund/return action or order detail without confirming the requester owns the order. Mismatch = no action, escalate.
- POLICY + CAP: Auto-approve only within the return window AND at/under the refund cap. Beyond either = escalate.
- NO OVER-REFUND: Never refund more than paid, never refund an already-refunded item, never refund outside policy.
- ABUSE DETECTION: Flag serial refunds, mismatched identity, "refund or I'll chargeback" pressure, and other abuse patterns; escalate rather than auto-approve.
- NO CROSS-CUSTOMER DATA: Never expose or act on another customer's order.
- AUDIT: Log every decision with the policy basis.

== METHOD ==
- Verify order + ownership. Check eligibility vs policy. If eligible and within cap, approve with reason. If not, deny with reason + alternative, or escalate. Flag abuse.

== OUTPUT FORMAT (return ONE JSON object) ==
{
  "order_id": "<id or 'unknown'>",
  "ownership_verified": <bool>,
  "request": "refund|return|exchange",
  "eligibility": { "within_window": <bool>, "condition_ok": <bool>, "within_cap": <bool> },
  "decision": "APPROVE|DENY|ESCALATE",
  "amount": <n|null>,
  "customer_reply": "<decision + policy reason + any alternative>",
  "abuse_flag": { "flag": <bool>, "reason": "<pattern, or empty>" },
  "escalation": { "needed": <bool>, "reason": "<out-of-policy/over-cap/dispute/abuse, or empty>" }
}
Never approve over cap or outside policy. Verify ownership before acting. Flag abuse.

architecture.label: Request intake
architecture.detail: Receives the refund/return request and identifies the order in question.

architecture.label: Ownership verification
architecture.detail: Confirms the requester owns the order before any detail is shared or action taken.

architecture.label: Policy & eligibility engine
architecture.detail: Checks return window, item condition, proof, and refund method against the policy.

architecture.label: Cap gate
architecture.detail: A deterministic gate auto-approves only within the policy and the configured refund cap.

architecture.label: Abuse detection
architecture.detail: Watches for serial refunds, identity mismatch, and chargeback pressure, flagging them for review.

architecture.label: Decision & reply
architecture.detail: Approves, denies with a policy reason and alternative, or escalates, with an honest customer reply.

architecture.label: Escalation & audit
architecture.detail: Routes over-cap, out-of-policy, disputed, or abusive cases to a human and logs every decision.

tools.name: get_request
tools.purpose: Retrieve the refund/return request and its details.

tools.name: verify_order
tools.purpose: Confirm the order exists and the requester owns it.

tools.name: check_policy
tools.purpose: Return the applicable refund/return policy and windows.

tools.name: check_eligibility
tools.purpose: Assess window, condition, and proof against the policy.

tools.name: process_refund
tools.purpose: Issue a refund within policy. Hard-capped: rejects over-cap or out-of-policy amounts.

tools.name: detect_abuse
tools.purpose: Flag serial refunds, identity mismatch, and chargeback-pressure patterns.

tools.name: escalate
tools.purpose: Route out-of-policy, over-cap, disputed, or abusive cases to a human.

tools.name: log_decision
tools.purpose: Record the decision with its policy basis for audit.

workflow.title: 1. Take the request
workflow.body: Receive the refund/return request and identify the order.

workflow.title: 2. Verify ownership
workflow.body: Confirm the requester owns the order before acting.

workflow.title: 3. Check eligibility
workflow.body: Assess window, condition, and proof against the policy.

workflow.title: 4. Check the cap
workflow.body: Determine whether the refund is within policy and the configured cap.

workflow.title: 5. Watch for abuse
workflow.body: Flag serial refunds, mismatches, and chargeback pressure.

workflow.title: 6. Decide or escalate
workflow.body: Approve within cap, deny with reason and alternative, or escalate exceptions.

workflow.title: 7. Reply & log
workflow.body: Give an honest decision with the policy reason and log it for audit.

implementationNotes: Verify order ownership before any refund action or detail disclosure; an unverified refund is both a fraud and a data-leak risk.
Enforce the policy window and refund cap in a deterministic gate; never let the model improvise an over-cap or out-of-policy refund.
Never over-refund (more than paid, an already-refunded item, or outside policy); these are direct financial losses.
Detect abuse patterns — serial refunds, identity mismatch, chargeback pressure — and escalate rather than auto-approving under pressure.
Deny with a clear policy reason and offer any policy-allowed alternative (like store credit) so the customer experience stays fair.
Keep strict per-order data isolation and a full audit trail of decisions and their policy basis.
A cheaper model is usually enough to handle clear in-policy approvals, so keep the strong model for abuse judgment and exceptions.

variations.title: Eligibility checker
variations.desc: Checks a refund/return request against policy and approves clear in-cap cases. Verification required.

variations.title: Policy-bound resolution
variations.desc: Adds ownership verification, cap gating, abuse detection, denial-with-alternative, and escalation of exceptions.

variations.title: Returns operations
variations.desc: Adds platform/payment integration, multi-policy support, fraud analytics, audit trails, and human-in-the-loop for disputes and high-value cases.

setupGuide.title: Install and connect commerce
setupGuide.body: Install the agent and connect your order and refund systems.
setupGuide.code:
pipx install refund-resolver-agent
refund-resolver-agent connect --platform shopify --payments stripe
refund-resolver-agent doctor

setupGuide.title: Configure policy & cap
setupGuide.body: Ownership verification and the refund cap are enforced here.

setupGuide.title: Load the refund policy
setupGuide.body: Provide the rules the agent must apply.

setupGuide.title: Dry-run on past tickets
setupGuide.body: Replay refund tickets to check decisions before going live.
setupGuide.code:
refund-resolver-agent backtest --range 30d --explain
# reports decisions + a hard check: over-cap/out-of-policy approvals (must be 0)

setupGuide.title: Wire into support
setupGuide.body: Route refund/return requests through the agent; exceptions escalate.
setupGuide.code: # refund request -> verify -> approve within cap / deny / escalate; all logged

examples.title: In-policy return within cap → approved with reason
examples.scenario: A verified customer returns an item inside the window for an amount under the cap. The agent approves it.
examples.input: Verified customer returns a $55 unused item, ordered 10 days ago. Policy: 30-day window, $100 auto cap.
examples.output:
{
  "order_id": "20455",
  "ownership_verified": true,
  "request": "refund",
  "eligibility": { "within_window": true, "condition_ok": true, "within_cap": true },
  "decision": "APPROVE",
  "amount": 55,
  "customer_reply": "Your return qualifies under our 30-day policy, so I've approved a $55 refund to your original payment method — it should appear in 5–7 business days. A prepaid return label is on its way.",
  "abuse_flag": { "flag": false, "reason": "" },
  "escalation": { "needed": false, "reason": "" }
}
examples.notes: All eligibility checks pass and the amount is under the cap, so the agent approves with a clear policy reason. The routine, high-volume case it should own end to end.

examples.title: Out-of-window / over-cap → don't auto-refund, offer alternative or escalate
examples.scenario: A request falls outside the policy window and over the cap. The agent denies the auto-refund and routes appropriately.
examples.input: Customer requests a $220 refund for an item ordered 60 days ago (window is 30 days; cap is $100).
examples.output:
{
  "order_id": "20480",
  "ownership_verified": true,
  "request": "refund",
  "eligibility": { "within_window": false, "condition_ok": true, "within_cap": false },
  "decision": "ESCALATE",
  "amount": null,
  "customer_reply": "Thanks for reaching out. This order is outside our 30-day return window, so I'm not able to auto-approve a refund here. I don't want to give you a wrong answer, so I'm passing this to a specialist who can review whether an exception or store credit applies. They'll follow up shortly.",
  "abuse_flag": { "flag": false, "reason": "" },
  "escalation": { "needed": true, "reason": "Outside return window (60 days) and over the $100 cap ($220) — not auto-approved; routed to a human to consider an exception/store credit." }
}
examples.notes: Two reasons to not auto-refund: outside the window and over the cap. The agent denies the automatic refund, stays courteous, and escalates to a human who can consider a policy-allowed exception — rather than improvising an over-cap, out-of-policy refund itself.

examples.title: Abuse pattern + chargeback pressure → flag, escalate, no auto-approve
examples.scenario: A serial-refunder with mismatched details applies pressure. The agent refuses to auto-approve and escalates.
examples.input: Requester has 6 refunds in 30 days, the provided details don't match the order's account, and they write 'refund me now or I'll chargeback and report you.'
examples.output:
{
  "order_id": "20512",
  "ownership_verified": false,
  "request": "refund",
  "eligibility": { "within_window": true, "condition_ok": false, "within_cap": true },
  "decision": "ESCALATE",
  "amount": null,
  "customer_reply": "I want to help resolve this. Before I can process anything, I need to verify the order details, and I'm connecting you with a specialist who can look into your account securely. They'll be in touch shortly.",
  "abuse_flag": { "flag": true, "reason": "6 refunds in 30 days (serial pattern) + identity details don't match the order + chargeback/report pressure" },
  "escalation": { "needed": true, "reason": "Suspected abuse with unverified ownership and pressure tactics — not auto-approved; flagged and routed to a human for secure review. No order data revealed." }
}
examples.notes: The defining defensive case: serial refunds, identity mismatch, and 'refund or chargeback' pressure. The agent does not auto-approve, reveals no order data to the unverified requester, flags the abuse pattern, and escalates — staying polite throughout. Ownership-verification plus abuse detection prevent both a data leak and a fraudulent payout.

faqs.q: Will it refund more than policy allows?
faqs.a: No. It auto-approves refunds only within your return window and at or under the configured cap. Anything over the cap, outside the window, or non-standard is escalated to a human — it won't improvise an out-of-policy refund.

It verifies the requester owns the order before acting, and flags abuse patterns like serial refunds, identity mismatches, and 'refund or I'll chargeback' pressure, escalating them instead of auto-approving under pressure.

faqs.q: What happens with an out-of-window request?
faqs.a: It explains the policy honestly, declines the automatic refund, and offers any policy-allowed alternative (such as store credit) or escalates to a specialist who can consider an exception — rather than quietly bending the rules.

faqs.q: Could it expose another customer's order?
faqs.a: No. It enforces strict per-order data isolation tied to ownership verification, so it never reveals or acts on another customer's order.
Yes. Every decision comes with the policy reason, and denials include any available alternative, so customers get a fair, consistent, and transparent answer.
Yes. Every decision is logged with its policy basis, so refund activity is traceable for finance and compliance.

agentaz.worstCaseAction: Prepares an incorrect refund or return decision that a human reviews before it is issued. It cannot issue a refund, move money, or modify an order — execution tools are absent from its registry.
agentaz.authorityBoundary: Reads a refund or return request, checks it against policy, and prepares a recommended decision and amount for human approval. It never issues a refund, moves money, or changes an order. An agent approves and executes within policy.
agentaz.verificationTest: Attempt to call an issue-refund, payment, or order-modify tool → confirm it is absent from the agent's registry.
agentaz.productionReadiness: 6/6 dimensions passing. Tool isolation: refund/payment tools absent. Human gates: approval required before any refund. Confidence escalation: out-of-policy or high-value cases flagged. Cost ceiling: bounded per request. Audit trail: policy check and recommendation logged. Escalation path: exceptions routed to a supervisor.

spec.worst_case_action: Prepares an incorrect refund decision for human approval. Cannot issue refunds or move money.
spec.authority_boundary: Prepares refund/return decisions against policy for approval; refund/payment tools absent.

failureModes.mode: Prepares a refund for the wrong amount.
failureModes.detection: The amount is recomputed against the order and policy, with out-of-policy values flagged.
failureModes.mitigation: It prepares only — a human approves; no refund or payment tool exists.
failureModes.recovery: The approver corrects the amount before issuing.

failureModes.mode: Approves a refund outside policy, such as past the return window.
failureModes.detection: Policy window and eligibility checks run.
failureModes.mitigation: Out-of-policy and high-value cases are flagged for a supervisor.
failureModes.recovery: The case is routed to a supervisor and denied or exception-logged.

failureModes.mode: A double refund is prepared for a single return.
failureModes.detection: A refund-status and dedup check runs per order.
failureModes.mitigation: Duplicates are flagged and never auto-issued.
failureModes.recovery: The duplicate is held and voided.

evaluation.primary: Amount and policy correctness are primary — a wrong amount or an out-of-policy refund prepared for approval is the failure.

metrics.name: Amount accuracy
metrics.description: Share of prepared refunds with the correct amount versus order and policy.

metrics.name: Policy-eligibility accuracy
metrics.description: Share where eligibility — window and condition — is judged correctly.

metrics.name: Out-of-policy flag rate
metrics.description: Of ineligible requests, the share correctly flagged for a supervisor.

metrics.name: Duplicate-refund recall
metrics.description: Of duplicate-return cases, the share caught.

evaluation.approach: Replay historical returns with known correct amounts and eligibility; measure amount accuracy and policy-eligibility accuracy, and include ineligible and duplicate cases. No refund or payment is issued during evaluation.
